title: Quiz: Classic McEliece NIST Standard
description: Test your understanding of Classic McEliece

questions.question: Which variant does Classic McEliece use internally?
questions.explanation: Classic McEliece uses the Niederreiter variant where the message is encoded as the error pattern and the ciphertext is the syndrome. This results in smaller ciphertexts.

questions.question: What is the approximate public key size for mceliece348864 (Category 1)?
questions.explanation: mceliece348864 has a public key of 261,120 bytes (~255 KB). This is the main drawback of Classic McEliece compared to lattice-based schemes.

questions.question: What does the 'f' suffix mean in parameter names like mceliece348864f?
questions.explanation: The 'f' variants use semi-systematic form, which allows faster key generation while maintaining the same security level.

questions.question: Why does Classic McEliece use implicit rejection in decapsulation?

options.text: To prevent attackers from detecting decoding failures

questions.explanation: Implicit rejection returns a deterministic but unpredictable value on failure instead of an error signal. This prevents 'decoding failure oracle' attacks that could leak key information.

questions.question: What is the ciphertext size for mceliece348864?
questions.explanation: Classic McEliece has very small ciphertexts - only 128 bytes for Category 1. This is much smaller than Kyber's ~1KB ciphertexts, partially offsetting the large public key.

questions.question: What transformation provides IND-CCA2 security for Classic McEliece?
questions.explanation: The Fujisaki-Okamoto (FO) transform converts an IND-CPA secure encryption scheme into an IND-CCA2 secure KEM by re-encrypting during decapsulation to verify correctness.

questions.question: How does Classic McEliece's security confidence compare to Kyber/ML-KEM?

options.text: Higher due to 45+ years of cryptanalysis

questions.explanation: Classic McEliece offers the highest security confidence because it has withstood 45+ years of cryptanalysis with no practical attacks found on binary Goppa codes. Kyber has only ~10 years of analysis.

questions.question: Classic McEliece is ideal for use cases where public key size is the primary constraint.
questions.explanation: False! Classic McEliece has very LARGE public keys (255 KB - 1.3 MB). It's ideal when maximum security confidence is needed and key size is acceptable, not when key size is the primary constraint.
